title: Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average
subtitle: Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.
caption:
Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average

Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.

The line chart tracks the seasonal cycle of Arctic sea ice extent across five distinct time periods, plotting the average area in millions of square kilometers against the twelve months of the year. The vertical axis ranges from 4 million to 16 million square kilometers, while the horizontal axis displays the progression from January through December. Five light blue lines represent decadal averages for the 1980s, 1990s, 2000s, 2010s, and 2020s, arranged in descending order of ice coverage. A separate black line traces the data for the year 2025. The data shows a consistent U-shaped pattern for every series, with maximum ice extent occurring in the early months and minimum extent in late summer. In the 1980s, the average extent peaked near 16 million square kilometers in March and fell to roughly 7.5 million square kilometers in September. By the 2020s, the peak had dropped to approximately 14 million square kilometers, and the September low fell below 5 million square kilometers. The 2025 line sits below all decadal averages for most of the year, reaching a low point near 4.5 million square kilometers in September. The gap between the 1980s line and the 2020s line is visible across all months, showing a reduction in ice area throughout the entire annual cycle. The lines for the 2010s and 2020s cluster closely together, particularly during the summer minimum months. The visual arrangement places the earliest decade at the top of the chart and the most recent decade near the bottom, illustrating a stepwise decline in sea ice extent over the recorded period.
